bins.name: State Authority Over Retail Load
bins.desc: OPSI argues that FERC lacks FPA authority over load interconnections because load connects through retail service providers outside federal jurisdiction. States are already effectively regulating large load interconnections, no discrimination has been shown, and unilateral federal assertion of jurisdiction would intrude on state retail authority.
quotes: OPSI, like the National Association of Regulatory Utility Commissioners ("NARUC"), underscores the importance of maintaining the long-established boundary between federal and state authority and urges the Commission to maintain the current jurisdictional framework.
nearly all load connects via a retail service provider or similar entity over which the Federal Power Act ("FPA") conveys no authority to the Commission.
The most expedient way to advance reliable and timely interconnection is to honor long-standing jurisdictional precepts recognizing state authority over retail load interconnections.

FERC should "preserve and affirm states' retail regulatory authority under the FPA, ensure that large load interconnections do not compromise grid reliability or impose undue costs on retail customers, and respect state tools for promoting system flexibility and equitable cost allocation."
The ANOPR provides no evidence that current state- or utility-administered interconnection processes are discriminatory.
utilities have no incentive to discriminate against large loads, which represent new retail sales and investment opportunities.

OPSI is concerned that, by permitting end use customers to connect directly to the transmission grid, the Commission will seemingly expand its jurisdiction well beyond that provided by the FPA, i.e., into retail service.

Rather than unilaterally imposing federal jurisdiction on these interconnections, FERC should pursue a cooperative approach -- potentially, developing voluntary best practices with states or other novel approaches to meet the moment -- without undermining state authority or established statutory frameworks.

bins.name: 20 MW Threshold Too Low
bins.desc: OPSI contends the proposed 20 MW applicability threshold is too low to produce meaningful efficiency gains and would conflict with PJM-region states that have set thresholds ranging from 25 to 100 MW.
quotes: the reforms in this docket should apply to new loads greater than 20 MW. This threshold is likely too small to make interconnection processes meaningfully more efficient and may overtake state-level proceedings where voltage thresholds have been set higher than this threshold. States in the PJM region are considering or have approved thresholds for large load additions ranging from 25 to 100 MW.

bins.name: Deter Speculative Interconnection
bins.desc: OPSI supports measures to ensure large loads bear their fair share of transmission costs and to deter harmful speculation in the interconnection queue.
quotes: It is imperative that large loads looking to interconnect bear their fair share of transmission costs and that the Commission deter harmful speculation as these loads seek to interconnect.

bins.name: Option to Build and State Siting
bins.desc: OPSI acknowledges the option-to-build concept but insists any such right must explicitly recognize that state siting and permitting authority remains absolute.
quotes: granting the customer an option to build must explicitly recognize that state siting and permitting authority is absolute.

bins.name: Protect Ratepayers and Reliability
bins.desc: OPSI supports measures protecting retail customers from costs they did not cause, preserving state prudency review rights, and ensuring resource adequacy obligations are clearly assigned when large loads are added faster than new generation can materialize.
quotes: FERC should "preserve and affirm states' retail regulatory authority under the FPA, ensure that large load interconnections do not compromise grid reliability or impose undue costs on retail customers, and respect state tools for promoting system flexibility and equitable cost allocation."
It is imperative that large loads looking to interconnect bear their fair share of transmission costs and that the Commission deter harmful speculation as these loads seek to interconnect.

FERC rules must not impede the state's traditional right to perform prudency reviews and conduct cost causation analysis necessary to protect local ratepayers from costs they did not cause, especially where transmission charges are based on withdrawal rights.
The Commission should clarify who is responsible for meeting resource adequacy and reliability obligations in both restructured and vertically integrated contexts.

bins.name: Joint Generation and Load Study
bins.desc: OPSI sees merit in studying co-located load and generation together, noting it could create incentives for large loads to bring their own generation resources.
quotes: If large load additions come with its own generation and FERC promotes the more efficient study of generation and load together, this could be an incentive for these loads to bring their own generation.

bins.name: Cost Causation Stays with States
bins.desc: OPSI argues that cost responsibility and cost causation analysis for large load interconnections is a retail ratemaking function within state jurisdiction. FERC rules must not block state prudency reviews or shift costs to ratepayers who did not cause them.
quotes: FERC should "preserve and affirm states' retail regulatory authority under the FPA, ensure that large load interconnections do not compromise grid reliability or impose undue costs on retail customers, and respect state tools for promoting system flexibility and equitable cost allocation."
It is imperative that large loads looking to interconnect bear their fair share of transmission costs and that the Commission deter harmful speculation as these loads seek to interconnect.

FERC rules must not impede the state's traditional right to perform prudency reviews and conduct cost causation analysis necessary to protect local ratepayers from costs they did not cause, especially where transmission charges are based on withdrawal rights.

bins.name: No Basis for Federal Study Process
bins.desc: OPSI finds no evidence that current state and utility interconnection study processes are discriminatory, undercutting the rationale for imposing a federal interconnection study regime on load.
quotes: The ANOPR provides no evidence that current state- or utility-administered interconnection processes are discriminatory.

If large load additions come with its own generation and FERC promotes the more efficient study of generation and load together, this could be an incentive for these loads to bring their own generation.

bins.name: Co-location Study as Incentive
bins.desc: OPSI supports the concept of studying load and co-located generation together as a way to encourage large loads to bring their own generation, potentially easing resource adequacy concerns.
quotes: If large load additions come with its own generation and FERC promotes the more efficient study of generation and load together, this could be an incentive for these loads to bring their own generation.

bins.name: PJM Region State Thresholds
bins.desc: OPSI notes that PJM-region states have set large load thresholds ranging from 25 to 100 MW, and describes PJM's ongoing Critical Issue Fast Path process for large load additions.
quotes: the reforms in this docket should apply to new loads greater than 20 MW. This threshold is likely too small to make interconnection processes meaningfully more efficient and may overtake state-level proceedings where voltage thresholds have been set higher than this threshold. States in the PJM region are considering or have approved thresholds for large load additions ranging from 25 to 100 MW.

bins.name: Cooperative Federal-State Approach
bins.desc: OPSI urges FERC to pursue voluntary best practices, joint hearings, and other cooperative mechanisms available under the FPA rather than unilaterally asserting jurisdiction over retail load interconnections.
quotes: OPSI, like the National Association of Regulatory Utility Commissioners ("NARUC"), underscores the importance of maintaining the long-established boundary between federal and state authority and urges the Commission to maintain the current jurisdictional framework.
The most expedient way to advance reliable and timely interconnection is to honor long-standing jurisdictional precepts recognizing state authority over retail load interconnections.

Rather than unilaterally imposing federal jurisdiction on these interconnections, FERC should pursue a cooperative approach -- potentially, developing voluntary best practices with states or other novel approaches to meet the moment -- without undermining state authority or established statutory frameworks.

bins.name: State Siting Authority Absolute
bins.desc: OPSI insists that any option-to-build provision must explicitly recognize that state siting and permitting authority is absolute and cannot be overridden by federal interconnection rules.
quotes: granting the customer an option to build must explicitly recognize that state siting and permitting authority is absolute.

bins.name: Resource Adequacy Gap Risk
bins.desc: OPSI warns that if large loads interconnect faster than new generation comes online, resource adequacy obligations become unclear, and the Commission must clarify who bears those obligations in both restructured and vertically integrated contexts.
quotes: The Commission should clarify who is responsible for meeting resource adequacy and reliability obligations in both restructured and vertically integrated contexts.
